steps.title: Identify the question type
steps.content: Support-the-claim question. The student's claim has two parts: (1) obedience to law in a healthy state must be as close to absolute as possible, and (2) even minor infractions should not be ignored. The correct quotation must address minor or unnoticed violations accumulating to harm the state.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
• ""When constructing his argument regarding the characteristics of a well-functioning government, Aristotle asserts that 'Transgression creeps in unperceived and at last ruins the state,' illustrating this idea with a comparison to frequent small expenditures slowly and almost imperceptibly chipping away at a fortune until it is ultimately depleted."": directly supports both parts — small/unnoticed infractions, if tolerated, accumulate until they ruin the state, so they cannot be ignored. ✓
  • ""When Aristotle writes on the necessity of avoiding corruption in government, he proposes that 'every state should be so administered and so regulated by law that its magistrates cannot possibly make money.' In particular, he thinks oligarchies are particularly susceptible to corruption through bribery."": about preventing financial corruption of officials, not about strict enforcement against minor citizen infractions. ✗
  • ""When Aristotle considers the health of constitutions, he states that 'Constitutions are preserved when their destroyers are at a distance, and sometimes also because they are near, for the fear of them makes the government keep in hand the constitution.' He holds that rulers who wish to see constitutions preserved must continually remind the populace of the dangers that would result from a constitutional collapse."": about external threats motivating vigilance, not about treating every minor infraction as significant. ✗
  • ""When contrasting different forms of government, Aristotle holds that 'oligarchies may last, not from any inherent stability in such forms of government, but because the rulers are on good terms both with the unenfranchised and with the governing classes.' That is, oligarchic leaders who wish to hold on to power will introduce members of disenfranchised classes into government in a participatory role."": about power-sharing in oligarchies to maintain stability, unrelated to absolute obedience or minor infractions. ✗
That matches the correct answer — A is correct.
